slides.title: Python's Built-In Power Tools
slides.body: Python ships with functions like `enumerate`, `zip`, `map`, and `filter` that make list processing **clean and expressive**. No more manual index juggling — just tell Python *what* you want.
slides.tip: Code that says what it does, not how to count, is called "Pythonic".

slides.title: enumerate() — Index + Value, Free
slides.body: Instead of maintaining a counter variable, `enumerate(sequence)` hands you **both the index and the value** in one go.
slides.tip: start=1 makes numbered lists feel natural to human readers.

slides.title: zip() — Pair Two Lists Together
slides.body: `zip(a, b)` acts like a jacket zipper — it pairs items from two lists **side by side**, one by one. Perfect for names + scores, keys + values, and more.
slides.code:
names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]
scores = [95, 82, 78]

for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f"{name}: {score}")
slides.tip: Gotcha: zip() stops at the shorter list — extra items are silently dropped.

slides.title: map() and filter() — Transform & Select
slides.body: `map(fn, seq)` runs a function on **every item**; `filter(fn, seq)` keeps only items where the function returns `True`. Both are lazy — wrap in `list()` to get results.
slides.code:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

evens = list(filter(lambda n: n % 2 == 0, numbers))
print(evens)
slides.tip: List comprehensions often read more clearly: [n for n in numbers if n % 2 == 0]

slides.title: sorted(), any(), all(), sum() — One-Liners
slides.body: `sorted(key=...)` sorts by any property; `sum()`, `min()`, `max()`, `any()`, and `all()` **collapse a whole list into a single answer** — no loop needed.
slides.code:
scores = [72, 85, 91, 60, 88]

print(sum(scores))                      # 396
print(any(s >= 90 for s in scores))    # True

slides.title: You've Got the Toolkit!
slides.body: These built-ins — `enumerate`, `zip`, `map`, `filter`, `sorted`, `any`, `all`, `sum` — are the vocabulary of **readable Python**. Mix and match them to process data in just a few clean lines.
slides.tip: When you reach for a manual counter or a nested loop, ask: is there a built-in for this?
